fact: September 14 is exactly one week before the autumn equinox, when day and night become perfectly equal!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime spider season - house spiders are frantically searching for mates and scurrying into homes at maximum speed.
Maple trees in Canada and northern US states start their color-changing magic right around now, with some leaves already flashing bright red.
The sun rises about 2 minutes later each day around September 14 in most places, making mornings noticeably darker than just a week before.
Monarch butterflies are in full migration mode, with millions of them flying up to 100 miles per day toward Mexico.
September 14 falls smack in the middle of harvest season, when pumpkins are rapidly growing up to 40 pounds per day in some patches.
This date marks peak acorn-dropping time for oak trees - squirrels are literally going nuts collecting up to 3,000 acorns each for winter.
In the Southern Hemisphere, September 14 brings the first real hints of spring warmth, with soil temperatures rising fast enough to wake up hibernating insects.
The moon phase around September 14 creates some of the year's most dramatic tides, especially when it coincides with a new or full moon.
Geese start honking their way south in massive V-formations, with some flocks containing over 1,000 birds flying at 40 mph.
September 14 sits right in the sweet spot when morning dew becomes heavy enough to make spider webs sparkle like tiny chandeliers at sunrise.
Apple trees hit their peak ripeness around this date, with the fruit containing maximum sugar content that makes them extra crunchy and sweet.
